data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Humana (HUM) After Its Steep Share Price Slide?
data.description:
• If you are looking at Humana and wondering whether the recent share price puts it in bargain territory or not, it helps to start with the facts behind the current valuation story.
  • Humana's share price most recently closed at US$165.43, with returns of a 7.7% decline over 7 days, a 5.7% decline over 30 days, a 37.5% decline year to date, a 33.7% decline over 1 year, a 65.5% decline over 3 years, and a 57.7% decline over 5 years.
  • Recent headlines around Humana have focused on how the share price performance compares with broader healthcare names and what that might imply for investor risk appetite. This context is prompting some investors to reassess whether the current level reflects sentiment, fundamentals, or a mix of both.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Humana has a value score of 5, which means it screens as undervalued on five of those checks. Below, we look at what that actually means using different valuation approaches, then finish with a framework that many investors find useful for understanding fair value in context.

Find out why Humana's -33.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Humana Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value.

For Humana, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $142.21m. Based on analyst inputs for the next few years and then Simply Wall St extrapolations beyond that, projected free cash flow rises into the multi billion range, reaching an estimated $4.66b by 2030. The ten year projections, all in dollars, are discounted back to today using Humana specific assumptions to reflect risk and the time value of money.

Pulling those discounted cash flows together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $965.79 per share, compared with the recent share price of $165.43. That implies the stock screens as 82.9% undervalued under this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Humana is undervalued by 82.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 48 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Humana.

Approach 2: Humana Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay per share with the earnings the business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars investors are paying for each dollar of earnings today.

In general, higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower, more cautious multiple. There is no single “right” P/E, which is why context matters.

Humana currently trades on a P/E of 16.72x. That sits below the Healthcare industry average P/E of 22.07x and below the peer group average of 20.89x, which suggests the market is applying a lower multiple than these broad benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what P/E might be appropriate for Humana once you factor in its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks. Because it is tailored to the company, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the overall industry, which may have very different characteristics.

For Humana, the Fair Ratio is 33.23x versus the current 16.72x, which indicates that the shares screen as undervalued on this P/E based approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Let us introduce you to Narratives, a simple tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that lets you attach your own story about Humana to concrete numbers like future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value. You can then compare that fair value with today’s price as new news or earnings arrive. One investor, for example, uses a higher fair value of US$348.00 built on faster growth and stronger margins. Another uses a lower fair value of US$146.00 based on tighter Medicare Advantage economics. Both views update automatically as fresh information comes in so you can see which story you agree with and how that lines up against the current share price.

For Humana, however, we will make it easy for you with previews of two leading Humana Narratives:

These are investor-written scenarios that tie specific assumptions on regulation, growth, margins and fair value back to concrete numbers. One leans more constructive on value at today’s price, and the other focuses on ongoing pressure around Medicare Advantage and earnings risk.

🐂 Humana Bull Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$212.87 per share

Undervalued versus the last close of US$165.43: about 22.3%

Revenue growth assumption: 8.70%

  • Sees Humana operating in a tougher regulatory climate where Medicare Advantage is under closer scrutiny, especially on risk adjustment and prior authorization, with less room for documentation errors.
  • Frames compliance as a core operating pillar, where data integrity, clinical justification and auditable systems are essential to managing audit risk and potential repayments.
  • Argues that large scale, if paired with stronger internal controls and proactive engagement with regulators, can support resilience even as oversight tightens across the industry.

🐻 Humana Bear Case

Overvalued versus the last close of US$165.43: about 13.3%

Revenue growth assumption: 9.10%

  • Highlights Humana’s heavy exposure to Medicare Advantage and the risk that policy changes, budget pressure and competition could weigh on reimbursement and earnings stability.
  • Points to rising healthcare costs, higher utilization and tech enabled competitors as possible sources of margin pressure if cost savings and efficiencies do not keep pace.
  • Uses one of the more cautious analyst fair value estimates, built on modest revenue growth, relatively low long term profit margins and a lower future P/E multiple to reflect execution and regulatory risk.

data.title: Rheinmetall Leans Into Pure Defense As Backlog Outlook Expands

  • This move marks a major shift in Rheinmetall’s business mix toward both land and maritime defense systems.

The planned focus on pure defense, away from civilian automotive activities, reshapes how investors may think about the company’s risk profile, revenue drivers and sensitivity to defense budgets.

For investors, the key questions are how this sharper defense focus and the Luerssen acquisition might change Rheinmetall’s earnings mix, cash flow visibility and capital needs over time. The guidance to grow the order backlog to €135b by 2026 also raises issues around execution capacity, supply chains and how quickly that backlog could convert into revenue and profits.

Approach 1: Chemed Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a business may generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the entire company could be worth now.

For Chemed, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is $329.0 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts through 2027, with Free Cash Flow of $379.1 million in that year. Simply Wall St then extrapolates cash flows out to 2035, with projected figures such as $326.2 million in 2026 and $476.1 million in 2035, all in $.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $717.69 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $401.87, this implies the stock is about 44.0% undervalued under these assumptions.

Approach 2: Chemed Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars of price you are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually call for a lower one.

Chemed currently trades on a P/E of 20.86x, compared with the Healthcare industry average of about 22.07x and a peer group average of 33.75x. This is a proprietary estimate of the P/E the company might attract given factors such as its earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile.
